sentences.text: Yes, he came much later that day.
I was there around maybe quarter to seven of my parents' house.
And it was late afternoon about when he came, so I was quite worried what that could be, you know, since there was the war.
And we heard that they were fighting at the bridges.
And my husband had to go over those bridges.
So he will tell that story.
I leave that to him.
And on the very first days, they were very, yeah, nerve wracking, I would say.
The people, of course, were all on the street and talking and talking.
And it was something, you know, so unexpectedly, really, in a way.
And the second day, it was not too good either.
All of a sudden we got the news that the water was poisoned.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And yeah, whoever drank the water, you don't know.
That went through, all from mouth to mouth.
Mouth to mouth.
And we quickly had water and boil it.
It was the best thing if there comes, you know, your life, boil the water and cool it then.
And at the time, we didn't have all those kinds of soda or whatever.
You know, we didn't have that.
So as we are thirsty, we drink water.
And that took a few hours.
And everybody was very nervous and looking if somebody drank the water and whatever.
And after a few hours, it just died off.
I think it was just something to try to create panic.
Whoever did that, we don't know.
But it was not true.
But for hours, people were you know, so afraid of their children, that it was poisoned.
But it wasn't so.
And then we had more bombings.
We heard on the third day, they had a bombing, because they were fighting so hard on the bridge, bridges.
And in the time they were fighting on the bridges, they bombed the [DUTCH] from the marines.
[DUTCH] was in Rotterdam.
They bombed that flat.
Yeah, for the headquarters.
They bombed that.
So they lost that.
And I'm sure they were even more mad at that time.
Those Marines, the people who have seen it, they said they got aligned on the bridges like cats.
All afraid of the deep water below.
And from all sides, they, they, they grabbed them, and then they fought, and they fought.
And they got not get a foot on the bridge, the Germans.
But they came right away in the morning, to help out, to defend Rotterdam.
Because they wanted the city.
They wanted the city, Rotterdam.
And they had a very rough, rough time.
And especially when they came closer to Rotterdam, the airplanes, they were bombing.
And the bombs went left and right and left and right.
And they were almost in the harbors to get there.
But it was so severe damaged, it sighs and it sighs and it sank, just before they left Rotterdam.
Just before they could leave the harbors, it sank.
It was sad that only third or the fourth day.
Must have been the third day.
[DUTCH], No, the first day.
The first day, sank already.
The bombing, I would say that was a-- was beautiful day, a day like this.
It was a little warmer, I think, and blue sky.
And we just had finished lunch.
And we heard the siren.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And I said again and again, because we had heard it so many times.
Oh again, when will it ever stop?
And we look out the window, and we saw one bomber and two.
And the bombs falling out.
It was right where we look out the window.
I said, oh God, it is really true.
A catastrophe is going to happen.
We all went to the window.
We all went from the windows, and it was going.
Terrible noise, bang, bang, bang, bang.
And we held each other.
You know, we, we were are all so tension, so afraid.
Because we really were between life and death.
The bombs fell only one block from us and to the city.
We didn't know it was that close.
It was just one block.
And I don't know how long it took.
But one after the other, and explosion after explosion.
And it was almost like your-- like the house was, was, was-- and I think we prayed and we held our own.
We held each other's hands.
They were cold.
And we look each other in the eye.
And will we live or will we not?
It was only a matter of seconds, we were dead or not, or buried under the thing.
And after a long time for us, finally it stopped.
We were alive.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we started to sob a little bit, of course.
We were so nervous and fed up.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And on the-- yeah, but really, when we saw the bombs starting, we looked out of the window at the streets.
And people also were running back and forth to try to find shelter.
Doors were open that people could go in.
And it was really in streets-- you know, I never forget.
I never forget.
With my mom, my dad, and and Cor was there too, of course.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my brother, two brothers and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one sister, yeah, two sisters, two sisters.
And then we, when that was over, we looked out the window again.
And people were running again, you, go to their homes.
They came out.
We had our door open to where people on stairway is sitting.
And people came all out and crying and rushing home.
And it was-- and burning and, oh, oh, oh.
So we went out too.
And we looked at one block.
We walked a block.
And there, sitting people on the grass already.
There was a little canal there.
The homes they lived in were bombed.
You saw  the flames behind the window panes.
And Cor and two brothers, they went out to see if they could help.
And they went to that area, where there was fire.
And they went into a building where the next building was on fire.
And they went into the basement or the first floor.
And there were people laying, with their legs under a beam.
Because were all beams fallen out.
And it was, was, a little room.
Well, we went-- after a  few days, we went back where we could have a little ferry.
And we could go back to our home.
And then we went.
It was about six or seven days after we went there, after the war.
Maybe two days after the war, we went home again.
And yeah, that  was, of course, such a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I couldn't say, such a difference.
You didn't know where to go yet.
You didn't know what is going to be tomorrow.
My husband, of course, was out of work, because his place was bombed down.
And yeah, right away, we tried to get some food.
Even people were even buying food right away, after the first day of war.
There were-- stores were full of people and getting food.
But we went, of course, after six days.
So we were trying to get some food, too.
But there was still food yet, you know, in the [DUTCH], in the first days.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I think-- yeah, we didn't get used to it.
You saw German soldiers walking around.
But they did not approach us, after it was there.
You know, there was a proclamation of the mayor that we were not supposed to be any hostile acts to the German soldiers.
Because it would cause only problems.
And it will-- and the mayor gave his life, if it happens, he gave his life, if it happens.
He said, if that happens in my city, I am the one who is responsible for it.
So that's what he thought in his proclamations.
That if you do it, it will cost my life.
So there sort of was no, no, no fighting anymore.
We only ignored them.
We ignored them.
And we saw them and didn't even look at them.
Did you get involved in anti-German activities?
Yes.
That was after-- let me see.
That was in '42.
Our son was born November 18.
And it was on an-- in a time of December, I think, let me see.
Yeah, it was in December.
And a brother of mine lived in the city.
He came to visit us on a Saturday afternoon.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he wanted to talk to us.
I said, well, what do you want?
He said, yeah, if you agree, both of you.
I said, what is it?
He said, I am, I am looking for a place for people to stay overnight for maybe for a week or so.
And they are bombed out or whatever.
And Cor and I look at each other.
And we said, are you in underground?
He said, yes.
The people are Jewish, parents with a young child.
They had been picked up.
And they got them out.
And they were looking for a hiding place.
And they had found a hiding place.
But then, just before they went to the hiding place, they got a warning from the underground-- don't.
That was dangerous.
So it was a trap.
They had a place for them.
They would have been trapped right in the place.
So they got out through the underground.
And then my brother didn't know anything more to do to us.
But I found out later, he helped people in his house already himself.
What was your brother's name?
Simon.
But we didn't know that at the time.
And we didn't know he was in the underground.
Only we found out now.
So when he said, if it is possible-- and of course, we discussed a lot of things, you know.
What they say, yes or no, and what can we do.
And he told us, you know, a lot of instructions if we want to.
So we decided.
He said, and I will bring them tonight, after dark.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So after he left, we really sat down.
And we are in it.
We are in.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And we didn't feel bad about it.
We even felt, yeah, that's what we're going to do.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we waited, and it was dark.
And finally, the doorbell rang.
And they came in.
They were very cold.
They had walked a long way in the cold.
Because they had no house.
And--
That was Max and the [DUTCH] and parents.
Max and [DUTCH] we called her.
Konyn.
Konyn, that is capital K-O-N-Y-N.
And it means rabbit.
And in Holland, a lot of Jewish people-- the last names are rabbit, like a hare and Konyn, or Stein, that were in Holland typical Jewish last names.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So, and--
Two and a half years old, an adorable little girl.
Adolfine, but we called her Dolfy.
And Adolfine, they were pretty relaxed with, because of Adolf Hitler.
So they wouldn't mind if they asked as Adolfine.
You know, they even said, yeah.
You know, that softens a little bit.
Later, yeah, yeah.
That was about-- not what they came in, of course.
Not when-- I didn't know if that's why they had chosen that name for the daughter.
Yeah, they called always Dolfy.
She was always called Dolfy.
That was the, the cute, little girl's name.
But the real name was Adolfine.
So they were not-- she was not named after him, after Hitler.
[LAUGHS] It was just a name, Adolfine, a family name, maybe.
And so, that was-- but one thing what is very interesting.
One neighbor was my brother, another brother of mine.
We had a dozen, little block of four-house, like a [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] duplex, but on four, four houses on one little block.
So in other words, the next neighbor was our-- was the same wall, on the side, like in a condo.
Now one side my-- another brother lived and was nice.
And the other side, a Nazi sympathizer lived.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we were very good with him.
He was an-- he was an idealist, an ideal man, who saw something in Nazism that was good.
We often had talks with him, before the war.
And for instance, he was-- that was true.
He said, for instance, how do they take care of older people?
That was, at that time, not good at all in the Netherlands.
People who were old who went in those nursing homes or retirement homes in those-- they were-- for instance, they had the men's section and the women's section.
So married couples were just at that time-- yeah.
And it was true.
We agreed with that.
But other things, we didn't agree.
But he was very open.
And he was just-- people who believed in that but was not a traitor.
He was not a traitor.
But still, you know, it was a big chance we took.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we realized that.
But we just knew, if he ever found out, he would not give it away.
We just knew that.
We were sure about that.
We hide the family, mostly we were in the front and the back.
Mostly we stayed in the back of the house.
And we had doors, little glass doors between.
So-- and we had always little curtains there, for, for decoration.
So it was they could not really look to the whole house, if they want to.
So that's where we stayed most of the time.
And it was winter anyway.
So we stayed in one room, we had not much fuel.
So that was really our headquarters, where we lived.
And they helped us.
And you mentioned once, did you get-- some compensation for helping?
They had some money.
And they paid us their food, let me say it this way.
But no, big things, as a reward, we never wanted that, of course.
And they did not even try it.
It was just we tried to keep them from the Nazis.
Precautions?
We were quiet, very quiet, in the beginning.
And we were lucky that girl was not a little whiner or what.
She was very lovely.
And you played and everything.
And we did not laugh loud or whatever or talk loud.
And after a few weeks, we got an idea.
Because, at that time, there were-- the Germans were at the shores of the Netherlands.
And they were building bunkers.
And they always expected, of course, a invasion from this side or the other side.
They always want to go to England.
But they knew also if they're going to go to England, they could go to the shores, too.
They were afraid that England was invaded the Netherlands and France, to get to there.
So they had to build those-- the bunkers built.
And people had to evacuate there.
And Max and [DUTCH], they lived in that area, maybe a half a mile or three quarters of a mile away.
So the house was occupied already by the Germans.
But we made up a story that they were evacuees.
And so we housed them, until there was another place available.
So it was only temporarily we would house them.
And we kept that up.
And pretty soon after that, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Simon was working on-- no identifications, false identifications for the people.
And he knew a way over there, to have it done.
At first, they had to-- got a-- the first one he did, was to get a star of the-- the David Star of the identification.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then it had to be renewed again, you know, with different names and totally renewed.
And he knew his way where they had it done.
It was all one cluster who did that.
Even the municipalities sometimes worked secretly along with them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then--
Yeah.
But first, you know, they couldn't go out, they never go out, you know.
And so when the star was off, they could move a little bit.
But it was not perfect yet, of course.
They needed new ones.
So then once in the afternoon, we were going to pick them up, Cor and I.
And we came to a house.
And I didn't know what the house was.
Simon was there.
He was waiting behind the door, when we ring on the door.
And he didn't say a word, little hello.
And we said hello, too.
So we understood, don't talk.
Don't talk and  don't ask questions.
So he-- we shake-- shook hands.
And when I  shook hands, I got something in my hands.
And  I put in my pocket.
And then I left again.
That were the, the new identifications.
And we left again.
And we came back.
And they were so relieved.
We looked at it.
You couldn't see any difference between a regular one, perfect, perfect.
But the name was now instead of Konyn was Bruin, Brown.
So it Max de Bruin and [DUTCH] de Bruin and Dolfy de Bruin.
And never, never mention Konyn again.
Never think of it.
They were de Bruin.
No, not many.
But we were lucky, it was winter.
And people didn't go out that much in the winter,  especially not during the war.
So we didn't have that much.
And also, we had bicycles, you know.
That was-- the only thing was neighbors.
But we didn't do it often, neighbors.
We didn't do that often.
So on one side, was good, my brother.
And the other side, we were very friendly.
But we didn't visit each other that much either.
And then when it became a little lighter, you know, and after-- no, after he got that identification card, they could go out  a little bit.
And he did a little bit on the bus.
And then he came back again.
And, yeah, I can make this story long.
We were very good live with each other.
We lived as one family.
It was one family.
We had a lot of fun sometimes.
But always, of course, on our-- always alert.
And once we thought there was something leaked out.
And they told us that.
And we tried right away, that was the very first two weeks, we tried right away to find another place.
I tried another place.
It couldn't be done.
And then I had an uncle and aunt living about 20 minutes from us.
And they lived more in a rural area.
I think maybe they want to do it.
When we went over there, and we told the story-- they didn't know we had people in the house.
And I told the story.
And I said, we can understand if you can't do it.
But just if you can do it, then we would be very happy with for the rest.
And they said, we cannot do it.
We said, we understand.
We cannot do it.
We understand.
Now, to make that open, I have in the end of my book-- but to tell that, we found out that my aunt and my uncle could not take them because they had weapons in their house stored.
But they couldn't tell us that either.
So we didn't know it from each other.
Sure.
They were ready to get-- to fight back again, you know.
And already when the English came or the Americans, when they were ready.
And then they could [DUTCH] war come out.
That is where all the weapons here.
My other brother had weapons too, stored in, in haystacks.
Yeah.
My brother, other brother did that.
Yeah.
He saw his parents taken away, from his front window, when they were living there.
They lived close by.
I never knew, because I didn't know the people myself before.
And they were taken away.
He had never heard of that.
He had a grown daughter, who lived in Belgium, by his first marriage.
She was picked up, never heard of.
So that were his relatives.
[DUTCH] family, her mother was hiding.
And her brother was hiding.
But they kept in contact.
I don't know where they called or whatever.
But they were-- it was a way that she could have the-- have some news or how her mother was doing.
It was not too far from us, not too far from us.
And they made it.
They made it.
What did the little girl, Dolfy, what-- what did she know of what was going on around her?
No, she didn't know.
No, two and a half years old, she didn't know.
She thought only, you know, she was living with us as a-- maybe first on vacation.
I don't know.
We didn't even-- that's a good question.
We didn't even told her that ourself.
She was right away-- as of course, she looked at the baby.
That was maybe one thing that was right away what to draw her attention-- the baby, the baby, the baby.
And as long as they were there, that baby [DUTCH] was her baby.
It was her little baby  brother, always she said.
So I think it was maybe, you know, you said so maybe it was one thing what took away the idea that she lived-- didn't live in her own house anymore.
It was a two and a half year old, you know.
OK.
